question: How can we configure port number in Angular?

answer.content:
1. ng serve --port <port number>
  2. Add configuration in angular.json file inside:  projects --> <app name> --> architect --> serve --> {"options": {"port": <port number>}}

• Improved User Experience: Routing allows users to navigate between different views or pages without actually loading a new HTML page from the server (SPA). Instead, the application dynamically updates the content in the browser by loading the necessary components and data based on the requested route.
  • Faster Page Transitions: With routing, only the necessary components and data for the requested route are loaded, resulting in faster page transitions.
  • Modularity and Maintainability: Routing encourages a modular structure for the application by separating it into different components associated with specific routes. This promotes code reusability and better maintainability. Each route can have its own component, making it easier to manage and update specific sections of the application independently.
  • Conditional Rendering and Dynamic Content: Routing enables conditional rendering of components based on the current route. This allows us to show or hide certain sections of the application based on the route path.
  • Route Parameters and Query Parameters: Routing supports passing route parameters and query parameters. Route parameters allow us to pass dynamic values within the URL, such as an ID or a username. Query parameters provide a way to pass additional data into the URL for filtering, sorting, or other purposes.
  • Route Guards and Security: Angular routing includes route guards, which is mechanism to control access to specific routes based on certain conditions. Route guards can be used for authentication, authorization, and other security-related purposes.
  • Nested Routes: Routing supports nested or child routes, allowing us to define a hierarchical navigation structure within the application.

question: Various States of Form in Angular.

answer.content:
1. Pristine: The value of form control has not been modified yet.
  2. Dirty: The value of form control has been changed.
  3. Untouched: The form control has not been blurred/focused yet.
  4. Touched: Form control has been touched(Blurred/focused).
  5. Valid: Form control values are valid.
  6. Invalid: All/any form control value is invalid.
  7. Pending: Validation of form control is in progress.
  8. Disabled: Control value is disabled.
Pristine vs Untouched: When form control is only focused/blurred/clicked it will be touched not dirty(Still Pristine). To make it dirty we need to modify the default value of controls. In Simple terms Dirty meaning whether we have modified the value or not and touched means whether we have touched the field or not.

The <ng-content> element specifies where to project content inside a component template.

ng-content is a directive that allows us to project content from a parent component into a child component. This is especially useful when we want to create reusable components that can accept dynamic content from their parent components.

The <ng-content> element is neither a component nor DOM element. Instead, it is a special placeholder that tells Angular where to render content.

Angular compiler processes all <ng-content> elements at build-time. We cannot insert, remove, or modify <ng-content> at run time.

We cannot add directives, styles, or arbitrary attributes to <ng-content>. (<ng-content *ngIf="isVisible"></ng-content> NOT ALLOWED).

We can have multiple <ng-content/> placeholders in single component. To pass data we can use CSS selectors.
The last one without any selector will be the default placeholder for all other elements coming from parent component.

Angular supports a special attribute ngProjectAs, that allows to specify a CSS selector on any element. 
It supports only static values and cannot be bound to dynamic expressions.

This is additional info passed using ng-content. This is Rating of product passed using ng-content. This is the Review section. This is generic info without selector This is list of recently.....

Additional Info:

Ratings:

Review:

Angular's <ng-template> element defines a template that is not rendered by default.
The <ng-template> element lets us declare a template fragment – a section of content that we can dynamically or programmatically render.

If we wrap content inside an <ng-template> without instructing Angular to render it, such content will not appear on a page.

We can get a reference to a template fragment in one of three ways:
  1. By declaring a template reference variable on the <ng-template> element.
  2. Referencing a template variable using queries.
  3. Injecting a template fragment.

This is a template fragment

This is one template fragment

 export class ComponentWithFragment { fragmentOne = viewChild>("fragmentOne"); }

This is one template fragment

 @Directive({ selector: "[myDirective]", }) export class MyDirective { private fragment = inject(TemplateRef); }

answer.title: Rendering Template fragments and passing data
answer.content:
We can render template fragments in 2 ways:
  1. Using ngTemplateOutlet
  2. Using ViewContainerRef

@let orderList = [{quantity: "2", color:"white", product:"Nike Shoe", type:"footwear", price: "2800"}]; @for (item of orderList; track $index) { List of Orders } Order Description: {{count}} {{color}} colored {{name}}. Price Details:

No Of Items: {{count}}

Price Per Unit: {{price}}

Total Price: {{count*price}}

question: What is RxJs and it's use cases.

answer.content:
RxJs stands for "Reactive extension for JavaScript".

It's a library written in JavaScript which helps us to manage asynchronous and event based programs using Observables.
It also helps in managing complex data flows, user events and HTTP requests reactively.

RxJs is based on functional programming fundamentals and implements design patterns like Observable pattern.

Key use cases of RxJs:
  1. Handling Asynchronous Events: Managing user input events such as click, mouse movements, keystroke by converting them in data streams and using concepts like debounce/filter.
  2. HTTP requests & API calls: Using Observables to manage API calls particularly handling the error situation, cancellation and retries.
  3. Sate Management: Managing complex application states throughout the application using Subjects.
  4. Combining multiple data stream: Merging/combining multiple asynchronous data stream in a single stream.
  5. Real-time data streams: Processing data from WebSocket or live data-stream.

[["RxJs","Promise","Signal"],["Streams multiple value/events.","Stream only one value then ends.","A single current value(continuous)."],["It's lazy, starts on subscription.","It's loaded eagerly.","It's synchronous, always ready."],["Can be cancelled if not required.","Can't cancel.","Can't cancel."],["Code cleanup required manually (unsubscribe).","Code cleanup not required.","Automatic code cleanup."],["Best for events that happen over time(search bar/WebSocket).","Best for simple one time HTTP Request where cancellation is not required.","Best for managing UI state/sharing data across components."]]

question: Subject, Behavior Subject, Replay Subject, Async Subject

answer.content:
- A Subject is a special type of Observable that acts as both an observable and an observer, allowing values to be multicast to multiple subscribers.

- A Subject can be used to create a multicast Observable, which means that multiple subscribers can receive the same values emitted by the Subject.
- A Subject can also be used to create a hot Observable, which means that the Observable starts emitting values as soon as it is created, regardless of whether there are any subscribers.

There are several types of Subjects in RxJs:
  1. Subject: The basic Subject that can be used to multicast values to multiple subscribers. 
                - Subscribers will only receive values emitted after they subscribe.
  2. Behavior Subject: A Subject that requires an initial value and emits the current value to new subscribers. 
                - Subscribers will receive the most recent value upon subscription, and then continue to receive subsequent values.
  3. Replay Subject: A Subject that can buffer a specified number of values and replay them to new subscribers.
  4. Async Subject: A Subject that only emits the last value to subscribers when the Observable completes. 
                - This is useful for scenarios where you want to emit last value after an asynchronous operation completes.

question: What is the definition of stream?

answer.content:
In RxJs Stream is a sequence of value/event happens over time.

We can consider stream as a continuous flow of data rather than one-time value.
Unlike array where all data present at once, stream emits data one by one at different point of time.

A stream can emit values indefinitely until it is explicitly completed or errors out.

Stream communicates with its observer through 3 types of signal:
  1. next(): A new value arrives in stream.
  2. error(): An error occurred and stream stops.
  3. complete(): Stream has finished, will not emit more values.

answer.title: Stream Vs Observable
answer.content: [["Stream","Observable"],["Stream is a sequence of data over time like flow of water.","Observable is blueprint or mechanism(water pipe itself), that defines how to create and manage such stream."],["It's Pull-based data model. (Consumer decides when to request/pull data from producer)","It's Push-based data model. (The producer decides when to send/push the data to consumer)"],["Designed for single consumption/subscriber.","Multiple subscriber can observe the same data."]]

The Observable is the stream of data, Observer is consumer that defines how to handle the data and Subscribe is the bridge that connects them and starts the data flow.

Observable: Represents a stream of data that can emit multiple values over time, either synchronously or asynchronously. They are "lazy," meaning they won't start producing values until something subscribes to them.

Observer: An object with callback functions that define how to react to the values emitted by an observable. The observer can have up to three methods: next(data), error(err), complete().

subscribe(): This method acts as the bridge connecting the observable and the observer. Calling subscribe() on an observable starts the execution of the observable's logic and begins the flow of data to the observer. It returns a subscription object which can be used to stop the data flow (Unsubscribe).

Different ways to handle Subscription Management:
  • async pipe automatically handles the subscription and unsubscription.
  • For observables that do not complete on their own (like user events or an infinite timer), unsubscription can be done in the ngOnDestroy() lifecycle hook.
  • In Angular v16+, we can use the takeUntilDestroyed() operator within a component or service to automatically manage unsubscription without implementing ngOnDestroy() explicitly.

question: Merge Map, Exhaust Map, Switch Map, Concat Map

answer.content:
These are called flattening operators. Flattening operators convert higher-order Observables (Observables emitting other Observables) into a single, flat Observable.

They all take an outer observable and map it to an inner observable.
  • Merge Map: It triggers an inner observable for every outer observable value immediately. Everything runs at same time parallelly.

    Use Case: Delete multiple values simultaneously, if user clicks rapidly on delete rows, we want to delete all rows parallelly.

  • Concat Map: It waits for current inner observable to complete before triggering next. It preserve the order.

    Use Case: Add records in sequential manner, If user added multiple skills and requirement is there to add in sequence.

  • Switch Map: If a new outer value arrives, it will cancel ongoing inner observable and triggers a new one. It only cares about last value.

    Use Case: Most commonly used for auto-search functionality if new value received in input box, we want result only for newer one.

  • Exhaust Map: If inner observable is incomplete it will ignore outer observable. It locks the stream.

    Use Case: Helpful in preventing the scenarios where user clicks multiple time on button and you want to process the first request.

answer.title: Merge Map
Concat Map
Switch Map
answer.content:
<form>
    <input type="text" name="searchField" 
    id="searchField" [formControl]="searchControl">
</form>

searchControl = new FormControl("");
searchResult$ = this.searchControl.valueChanges.pipe(
  // Only proceed if new value is different
  distinctUntilChanged(),

  switchMap(val => this.searchApi(val as string).pipe(
    catchError(() => "")
  ))
);

searchApi(val: string): Observable<any> {
  console.log("Search API-", val)
  return new Observable((c) => {
    setTimeout(() => {
      c.next(`Searching for ${val}.`)
    }, 3000)
  });
}

//Console output
Search API- c
Search API- co
Search API- cow

Searching for cow.

answer.title: Exhaust Map

answer.content:
All are flattening operators, key difference between these and the map versions(e.g. mergeMap()) is that these operators don't perform mapping.

They will only do flattening for observables available in stream.

Technically every map operator is combination of map() and all operator
e.g. mergeMap() = map() + mergeAll()

  • mergeAll(): It subscribes to every inner observable as soon as they arrive parallelly.
  • concatAll(): It subscribes to every inner observable one after another in sequential manner.
  • switchAll(): As soon as new observable will arrive it will cancel previous subscription and starts the new one.
  • exhaustAll(): It ignores new observable until on going one is not completed.

question: combineLatest, forkJoin, concat, merge, race, zip

answer.content: These are known as combination operators, we use them when we need to join information from multiple observables.

answer.title: combineLatest()
forkJoin()
concat()
merge()
zip()
race()
answer.content:
/**
 * race(): Subscribes to multiple source Observables 
 * and emits values from the first Observable to emit a value. 
 * All other Observables are then unsubscribed.
 * 
 * Which ever observable will emit first,
 * will get subscribed, other Observables 
 * are then unsubscribed.
 */

race(
  this.age$,
  this.name$,
).subscribe(val => console.log(val));

//Console Output
Aman
Ram
Joy
//2nd observable got ignored

@defer block in Angular is a template syntax feature introduced in angular 17, it allows us to lazy-load parts of template(component, directive, pipe and their CSS) on conditional basis.

This helps in reducing initial bundle size and improve performance by deferring the loading of code which is not necessary for initial page view.

The code for component, directive, pipe inside @defer block is split into a separate JavaScript file and loaded only when required, after the rest of the template has been rendered.

This is a one-time operation the @defer block does not revert to the placeholder if the condition changes to a falsy value.

Conditions for the dependencies within a @defer block to be deferred:
  • They must be standalone: non-standalone dependencies cannot be deferred, they will get loaded eagerly.
  • They cannot be referenced outside @defer within same file: If they are  referenced outside or referenced within ViewChild queries they will be eagerly loaded.
Note: Transitive dependencies (dependencies of dependency) of the component/directive/pipe used in @defer do not strictly need to be standalone.

Different stages of deferred loading:
  • @defer: This is the primary block that defines the section of content that is lazily loaded. By default, it loaded when the browser state becomes idle.
  • @placeholder: This is an optional block used to show the content before @defer block is triggered.
  • @loading: This is an optional block that allows us to declare content, that is shown while deferred dependencies are loading. It replaces @placeholder once loading is triggered.
  • @error: This is an optional block used to show template if deferred loading fails.

answer.title: Controlling deferred content loading with triggers/condition
answer.content:
We can specify triggers when angular loads and displays deferred content.

Multiple event triggers can be defined by separating with ; and will be evaluated as OR condition.

There are two types of trigger on (Some inbuilt events/conditions) and when(It accepts custom conditional expression).

The available on triggers are:
  • idle: This trigger loads the content once browser has reached an idle state.
    - This is the default behavior of defer block.
  • viewport: This trigger loads the content when specified content enters the viewport.
    - By default defer watches for the placeholder to enter the viewport. Placeholders used this way must have a single root element.
    - Alternatively we can specify a template reference variable, that is being watched to enter viewport to trigger loading.
  • interaction: This trigger loads the content when user interacts with the specified element.
    - By default placeholder acts as interaction element.
    - Alternatively we can specify template reference for interaction.
  • hover: This trigger loads the content when it has been hovered.
    - By default placeholder acts as interaction element.
    - Alternatively we can specify template reference for interaction.
  • immediate: This trigger loads the content immediately. This means deferred block will be loaded as soon as all other non-deferred content finishes rendering.
  • timer: This trigger loads the content after a specified duration. Duration parameter must be defined in (ms) or (s).
The when trigger accepts a custom conditional expression, it loads the content when it meets the condition.

View more

@defer(on viewport(viewMore)) {

This is lazily loaded data.

} @placeholder {

This is placeholder

} @defer(on viewport({rootMargin: '100px', threshold: 0.5})) {

This is lazily loaded data.

} @placeholder {

This is placeholder

}

Last Item

@defer(on viewport({trigger: lastitem, rootMargin: '100px', threshold: 0.5})) {

This is lazily loaded data.

}

View more

@defer(on interaction(viewMore)) {

This is lazily loaded data.

} @placeholder {

This is placeholder

}

answer.title: Prefetching data with prefetch and few important points.
answer.content:
We can optionally specify a prefetch trigger, this trigger lets us load JS associated with the @defer block before deferred content is shown.

@defer (on interaction; prefetch on idle) {
      <p>Content</p>
}

NgModule based dependencies are not deferred and are included in the eagerly loaded bundle.

When Hot Module Reload(HMR) is active, all @defer block chunks are fetched eagerly. To restore standard trigger behavior we need to disable HMR by serving application with --no-hmr flag.

In case of application on server (SSR or SSG), defer blocks load their @placeholder eagerly and trigger are not invoked. On the client, the content of the placeholder is hydrated and triggers are activated.


Things to avoid:
- Avoid cascading loads with nested @defer, if nested defer are there they should have different triggers.
- Avoid deferring content which are visible in user's viewport on initial load.
- Keep accessibility on mind, ensure that changes are announced to userwhen transition occurs.
